evidence: `importerRuntime` composes three embedded runtime bags and then repeatedly re-slices them (`buildImporterInitRuntime`, `buildImporterWorkflowRuntime`, `resolveImporterRuntimeFor*`), creating indirection layers without domain-level behavior.

`workflowRuntime` embeds `dockerOrchestration.RuntimeContainerOps`, `RuntimeUrbitOps`, and `RuntimeSnapshotOps`, while also carrying unrelated transition/timing callbacks, so one abstraction surface spans multiple concerns.

`startupInitRuntime`/`StartupRuntime` and `broadcast` lifecycle wrappers add multiple pass-through methods (`LoadStartramRegionsWithRuntime` -> `LoadStartramRegionsWithRuntimeState`, `runBroadcastTick` -> `runBroadcastTickWithRuntime`) that mostly rename calls.

Default runtime constructors hard-wire concrete dependencies across package boundaries: `importer.defaultImporterRuntime()` instantiates docker orchestration runtime and event runtime, and wires shipworkflow callbacks directly.
`shipworkflow.newWorkflowRuntime()` also constructs its own orchestration runtime and default event runtime, duplicating dependency ownership and coupling runtime composition to multiple subsystems.

`startuporchestrator.startupInitDefaultRuntime()` imports and binds concrete init/start functions from many packages (config/auth/router/system/importer/broadcast/docker), concentrating dependency direction in one package with no boundary interface.

holistic_context.structure.directory_profiles reports large flat directories for this batch scope: goseg/shipworkflow/ (51 files), goseg/config/ (41 files), goseg/docker/orchestration/ (34 files), each exceeding the >10 mixed-concern split threshold.

goseg/shipworkflow/doc.go states the package should stay focused on ship domain flows, but goseg/shipworkflow/newship_service.go combines transport parsing, patp validation, drive resolution, transition publication, and goroutine launch, while goseg/shipworkflow/newship.go performs runtime wiring across docker/network/config/startram.

goseg/config/config.go is partly a runtimecontext facade (BasePath/Architecture/DebugMode getters and Set* wrappers) while goseg/config/bootstrap.go owns process initialization and global mutable state, indicating boundary overlap between config domain APIs and runtime context plumbing.

goseg/docker/orchestration/container_bridge.go is primarily a bridge layer that constructs container.* runtimes and merges seams, yet remains in a broad orchestration root already containing many unrelated concerns; goseg/docker/orchestration/container_config.go also centralizes container-type dispatch in the same flat layer.
holistic_context.structure.coupling_matrix and root_files are null in this packet, so directional-coupling and root-straggler thresholds cannot be validated from packet metrics for this batch.

Config shape is modeled in parallel in two places: section/value structs in goseg/structs/configs.go (ConnectivityConfig, RuntimeConfig, StartramConfig, AuthSessionConfig, PenpaiConfig) and patch structs/registry in goseg/config/config_update_schema.go (ConnectivityPatch, RuntimePatch, StartramPatch, AuthSessionPatch, PenpaiPatch, configPatchField registry).

WiFi runtime combines multiple concerns in one type/file: nmcli parsing DSL (split/parse helpers), command execution orchestration, wifi-client operations, interface toggling, and sysctl captive-rule mutation in goseg/system/wifi_runtime_methods.go.

Tests repeatedly need package-global reset/rebinding scaffolding (e.g., resetExporterState in goseg/exporter/exporter_test.go, resetUploadSessionsForTest/authorizeTokenIDsForTest in goseg/importer/importer_test.go, resetParameterGlobalsForTest/resetRouterGlobalsForTest in goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint_test.go), indicating production seams are global-state oriented rather than instance-oriented.

Runtime seam definitions are fragmented into many function-bag structs in goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go, and tests in goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator_test.go and goseg/main_test.go build large ad-hoc runtime compositions to exercise behavior.

findings.summary: Importer runtime modeling is over-layered: embedded callback bags are repeatedly projected into narrower bags, adding indirection without adding policy.
evidence: `importerRuntime` embeds `importerStorageRuntime`, `importerUploadRuntime`, and `importerProvisionRuntime`, then `resolveImporterRuntimeFor*` repeatedly validates and converts to `importerInitRuntime`, `importerWorkflowRuntime`, and `importerPublishRuntime`.

Many call sites take `runtime ...importerRuntime` and immediately call a resolver (`resolveImporterRuntimeForUpload`, `resolveImporterRuntimeForWorkflow`, `resolveImporterRuntimeForPublish`), making call depth higher while behavior remains mostly pass-through.
`workflowRuntime` and orchestration runtime bags follow the same pattern, indicating systemic abstraction inflation rather than a single local need.
findings.suggestion: Replace bag-projection flow with explicit role interfaces (e.g., `UploadAuthorizer`, `Provisioner`, `TransitionPublisher`) passed directly to the operations that need them; keep one root composition point and remove repeated runtime conversion helpers.

findings.summary: Single runtime structs mix unrelated concerns, reducing interface honesty and forcing consumers to depend on methods they do not need.
evidence: `workflowRuntime` includes transition emission, sleeping policy, DNS resolution, event broker, and embedded orchestration ops in one type.
`startupInitRuntime` combines config/auth/router init callbacks, host-network checks, and storage/swap concerns in one composed runtime surface.
Validation is group-tag based (`runtime:"..."`) rather than type-level domain contracts, so the abstraction boundary is declarative metadata instead of strongly separated APIs.
findings.suggestion: Split runtime types by use-case boundary (startup-init, upload-auth, provisioning, transition-publish) and expose narrow constructor-level dependencies; keep tagging only for tests where unavoidable.

findings.summary: Broadcast lifecycle introduces wrapper chains that rename behavior but add little leverage, increasing call-chain depth and maintenance cost.
evidence: `LoadStartramRegionsWithRuntime` delegates directly to `LoadStartramRegionsWithRuntimeState` after nil checks; same pattern appears in bootstrap and reload paths.
`runBroadcastTick` delegates to `runBroadcastTickWithRuntime(newBroadcastLoopRuntimeWithDefault())`, and `resolveBroadcastLoopRuntime` just returns `runtime.stateRuntime`.
Multiple wrapper layers perform repeated nil/default checks around the same underlying operations.
findings.suggestion: Collapse duplicate wrappers into a single exported API per behavior with one normalization step; keep internal helpers only where they add distinct policy or error semantics.

findings.summary: Dependency wiring is distributed across subsystems via concrete default constructors, creating hidden coupling and brittle dependency ownership.
evidence: `importer.defaultImporterRuntime()` instantiates orchestration runtime and event runtime and directly binds shipworkflow dispatch behavior.
`shipworkflow.newWorkflowRuntime()` separately creates its own orchestration runtime and default event runtime instead of receiving shared dependencies from a composition root.
`startupInitDefaultRuntime()` and `main` each construct concrete startup paths, so dependency ownership is spread across packages instead of centralized.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single composition root (e.g., bootstrap container) that constructs shared runtimes/event brokers once and injects them downward; convert package-level default constructors to thin adapters over injected dependencies.

findings.summary: New-ship ingress and provisioning composition live in an overloaded top-level shipworkflow namespace instead of focused subfolders.
evidence: goseg/shipworkflow/ has 51 files in directory_profiles, exceeding the flat-directory cohesion threshold.
goseg/shipworkflow/newship_service.go:26-151 includes inbound message dispatch, key decoding, drive preparation, patp normalization/validation, and async provisioning trigger in one top-level file.

goseg/shipworkflow/newship.go:21-57 wires provisioning runtime dependencies from config, docker/network, shipcleanup, shipcreator, and startram, indicating composition concerns mixed into the same namespace as inbound handlers.
goseg/shipworkflow/doc.go:1-6 declares intent to keep package focused, but file placement currently mixes ingress/validation/runtime wiring in root.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create `goseg/shipworkflow/newship/ingress`, `goseg/shipworkflow/newship/validation`, and `goseg/shipworkflow/newship/runtime`; (2) move `HandleNewShip`/`HandleNewShipBoot` into `ingress`, move `normalizePatp`/`isValidPatp` into `validation`, move `newShipProvisioningRuntime` wiring into `runtime`; (3) keep root-level compatibility wrappers in `newship.go`/`newship_service.go` that delegate to new packages; (4) update imports with `rg "HandleNewShip|HandleNewShipBoot|NormalizePatp|ProvisionShip" goseg/shipworkflow -n` and `goimports -w goseg/shipworkflow`; (5) validate with `go test ./goseg/shipworkflow/...` then `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: Config root package mixes domain config APIs with runtime context/process bootstrap ownership, weakening directory boundaries.
evidence: goseg/config/ has 41 files in directory_profiles, and already contains focused subpackages (`runtimecontext`, `startramstate`, `wireguard*`) while core root files still aggregate heterogeneous responsibilities.
goseg/config/bootstrap.go:18-34 and 44-129 owns global state, initialization lifecycle, filesystem path helpers, and runtime flags setup.
goseg/config/config.go:92-160 largely forwards to `runtimecontext` getters/setters, while same file also defines multiple snapshot/domain settings types, indicating cross-boundary placement.
goseg/config/bootstrap_test.go only exercises path/debug helper behavior from bootstrap, reinforcing that runtime-context concerns are still anchored in root config.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) add `goseg/config/bootstrap/` for initialization lifecycle and `goseg/config/contextapi/` (or reuse `runtimecontext` facade package) for path/debug/arch adapters; (2) move bootstrap lifecycle functions (`Initialize`, `initializeConfig`, path/key helpers) first, then move runtimecontext pass-through getters/setters from `config.go`; (3) leave `config` package as a thin public facade re-exporting stable entry points; (4) update call sites via `rg "config\.(Initialize|BasePath|SetBasePath|DebugMode|SetDebugMode)" goseg -n` and run `goimports -w goseg/config goseg/...`; (5) validate with `go test ./goseg/config/...` and `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: Container runtime bridge and container-type dispatch remain in a crowded orchestration root instead of a dedicated container composition boundary.
evidence: goseg/docker/orchestration/ has 34 files in directory_profiles, while a dedicated `goseg/docker/orchestration/container/` subpackage already exists.

goseg/docker/orchestration/container_bridge.go:16-195 is a bridge/composition layer that primarily creates and merges `container.LlamaRuntime`, `container.NetdataRuntime`, and `container.MinioRuntime` templates.

goseg/docker/orchestration/container_config.go:13-61 centralizes container-type routing and builder dispatch, tightly related to container composition mechanics but placed in broad root orchestration package.
goseg/docker/orchestration/container_bridge_test.go:5-8 is a placeholder smoke test, so package-boundary expectations for this bridge layer are not asserted.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create `goseg/docker/orchestration/containerbridge/` (or fold into `goseg/docker/orchestration/container/compose`) and move `container_bridge.go` plus `container_config.go` there; (2) keep temporary forwarding functions in root orchestration to preserve call sites; (3) migrate tests by replacing placeholder smoke with behavior tests in the new package; (4) update imports using `rg "ContainerConfigForType|llamaRuntimeFromDocker|netdataRuntimeFromDocker|minioRuntimeFromDocker" goseg -n` and `goimports -w goseg/docker/orchestration/...`; (5) validate with `go test ./goseg/docker/orchestration/...` then `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: Contract-family definitions are split across parallel package shapes with different schemas, obscuring the canonical ownership boundary for governance contracts.
evidence: `family_specs_test.go` uses `contractFamilyEntrySpec` and governance metadata in package `contracts`.
`protocolfamily/specs.go` defines its own `ActionSpec` (Namespace/Action/ContractID/Owner).
`startramfamily/specs.go` defines another `ContractSpec` shape (ID/Name/Description/Message/Owner), creating parallel schemas for closely related contract catalog concerns.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: Stage 1 create `goseg/protocol/contracts/families/` with a single shared spec type plus per-family data files (`families/protocol_specs.go`, `families/startram_specs.go`); Stage 2 move existing constants/data from `protocolfamily/specs.go` and `startramfamily/specs.go` into that folder and remove duplicate struct types; Stage 3 update imports in tests and catalog builders to consume only the unified family package; Stage 4 run `cd goseg && gofmt ./protocol/contracts/... && go test ./protocol/contracts/... ./uploadsvc/...` to validate import updates and compatibility checks.

findings.summary: Contract governance quality gates are isolated under protocol contracts, while producer-side contract behavior is tested separately, weakening navigability of ownership-to-validation paths.
evidence: `fixtures_test.go` enforces canonical binding fixtures under `protocol/contracts/conformance`.
`actions_test.go` in `uploadsvc` validates action parsing only and has no direct governance/conformance linkage in the same folder.
A contributor changing upload action contracts must traverse separate directories to understand contract policy vs runtime action behavior.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: Stage 1 introduce `goseg/protocol/contracts/families/upload/` (or `goseg/uploadsvc/contracts/`) as the producer-facing contract boundary; Stage 2 move/add upload contract conformance tests adjacent to upload contract declarations while retaining shared validators in `protocol/contracts/conformance`; Stage 3 keep package imports one-directional (producer contracts -> shared conformance helpers); Stage 4 run `cd goseg && go test ./protocol/contracts/... ./uploadsvc/...` and `go list -deps ./uploadsvc/... ./protocol/contracts/...` to confirm import updates and boundary clarity.

findings.summary: Top-level quality-gate documentation is too coarse to reflect the split contract-governance package layout, increasing docs-vs-runtime navigation drift.
evidence: `README.md` backend gate lists `go test ./...` but does not identify contract-governance entrypoints or family/conformance package boundaries.
Governance checks are concentrated in specific contract test files (`family_specs_test.go`, `conformance/fixtures_test.go`) that are not discoverable from the documented gate structure.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: Stage 1 add a contracts section in `README.md` pointing to target folders (`goseg/protocol/contracts/`, `goseg/protocol/contracts/conformance/`, family-spec location after consolidation); Stage 2 document move order for any family package relocation before test commands; Stage 3 add explicit validation commands (`cd goseg && go test ./protocol/contracts/... ./uploadsvc/...`) alongside `go test ./...`; Stage 4 verify docs/runtime alignment by running the documented commands exactly and confirming they cover contract compatibility checks.

findings.summary: Configuration domain is duplicated across core structs and patch-schema registry, creating a systemic drift surface.
evidence: goseg/config/config_update_schema.go:14-82 defines section patch structs and ConfPatch composition that mirror config sections.
goseg/structs/configs.go:90-154 and 168-210 defines section access/update methods and section structs for the same domains.

Adding/changing a config key requires coordinated edits across patch types, parser/apply registry, base structs, and merge tests (goseg/config/config_test.go), indicating one domain represented by multiple manual schemas.
findings.suggestion: Generate patch registration from a single canonical config schema (or use a typed field descriptor table shared by structs and update parsing) so keys, types, and section ownership are declared once and reused for parse/apply/merge paths.

findings.summary: wifiRuntime file mixes parsing, transport, device control, and host sysctl policy in one cohesion unit.
evidence: goseg/system/wifi_runtime_methods.go:66-155 implements nmcli parsing and parse-error modeling.
The same file also contains transport and control methods (runNmcli/listSSIDs/connectedSSID/connect/disconnect/toggleDevice) at lines 182-316.
Host network policy mutation (applyCaptiveRules sysctl writes) is co-located at lines 318-329, a distinct operational concern from wifi scanning/connection.
findings.suggestion: Split into focused collaborators: nmcli parser module, wifi device control adapter, and captive-network policy applier; compose them in a small runtime facade to keep call sites stable while reducing per-file responsibility breadth.

findings.summary: A repeated global-mutation test pattern indicates hidden coupling and weak local boundaries.
evidence: goseg/exporter/exporter_test.go:53-72 resets many package-level function vars and shared maps before each test.
goseg/importer/importer_test.go:64-132 resets shared upload session globals and directory globals, then patches runtime callbacks per test.
goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint_test.go:113-221 repeatedly restores package globals and command hooks across different test groups.
findings.suggestion: Move these packages toward explicit runtime/context instances passed into handlers/services, then provide shared test builders for those runtimes; this removes per-test global reset choreography and narrows fixture scope.

findings.summary: Orchestration runtime seams are spread across many loosely-related function bags, increasing composition complexity.
evidence: goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go:17-173 defines numerous operation structs (container/snapshot/health/startram/startup/urbit/context/command/wireguard/minio/etc.) with overlapping lifecycle ownership.
Tests construct multi-part runtime overrides (e.g., withStartBackgroundServicesRuntime in goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator_test.go:24-47 and callback fan-out at 180-189).
main test runtime options likewise compose nested runtime fragments (goseg/main_test.go:34-61), showing consumer-side complexity caused by seam fragmentation.
findings.suggestion: Introduce higher-level domain ports with narrower contracts (e.g., startup port, ship lifecycle port, network port) and keep low-level callback bags internal; expose builders that validate required dependencies once.
